text: Carton brought the conversation to a close at this point by rising to assist him with his outer coat. “But you,” said Mr. Lorry, returning to the subject, “you are young.”
source_text: Carton terminated the conversation here, by rising to help him on with his outer coat; “But you,” said Mr. Lorry, reverting to the theme, “you are young.”

text: There was no reply. I had made my first point! For the first time, the smith was not up and ready. The company noticed it. A good effect.
source_text: There was no answer. I had scored my first point! For the first time, the smith wasn't up and ready. The company noticed it. Good effect.

text: “There is one curious thing,” said one of the sons, a small old man very like his father; “that is, none of his children have ever wandered away; we have all stayed”—he might almost have said stayed seated—“all our lives, close about him.”
source_text: “There's one interesting thing,” said one of the sons, a little old man much like his father; “that is, that none of his children have ever gone meandering off; we've all remained”--he might almost have said remained seated--“all our lives, right about him.”

text: “Is it not impressive, Mr. Darnay?” Lucie asked. “There have been evenings when I have sat here till I almost imagined—but even the shadow of such a foolish thought gives me a shiver to-night, when everything is so dark and solemn—”
source_text: “Is it not impressive, Mr. Darnay?” asked Lucie. “Sometimes, I have sat here of an evening, until I have fancied--but even the shade of a foolish fancy makes me shudder to-night, when all is so black and solemn--”

text: “And would you believe it,” she went on, turning back to Mrs. Britling, “that man Hickson stood there behind his counter—where I have dealt with him for years—and positively refused to let me have more than a dozen tins of sardines. Refused! Outright!”
source_text: "And would you believe me," she went on, going back to Mrs. Britling, "that man Hickson stood behind his counter--where I've dealt with him for _years_, and refused absolutely to let me have more than a dozen tins of sardines. _Refused!_ Point blank!

text: He waited and waited, and the clock struck twelve; yet Doctor Manette did not return. Mr. Lorry came back, and found no news of him, and brought none. Where could he be?
source_text: He waited and waited, and the clock struck twelve; but Doctor Manette did not come back. Mr. Lorry returned, and found no tidings of him, and brought none. Where could he be?

text: “No. And she had been getting on so nicely too. She’s scarcely any better now than she was at Christmas again. She keeps on asking whether you have come, and whether there’s any news, till one is quite wearied out with answering her. Shall I go and tell her you’ve come?”
source_text: “No. And getting on so nicely as she was too. She’s but very little better now again than she was at Christmas. She keeps on asking if you be come, and if there’s news, till one’s wearied out wi’ answering her. Shall I go and say you’ve come?”

text: “I have delivered that letter,” Charles Darnay said to Mr. Lorry. “I could not have agreed to your being entrusted with any written reply, but perhaps you will accept one given by word of mouth?”
source_text: “I have delivered that letter,” said Charles Darnay to Mr. Lorry. “I would not consent to your being charged with any written answer, but perhaps you will take a verbal one?”

text: “Ah! so you’ve changed your mind, have you?” growled Sikes, noting the tear that quivered in her eye. “So much the better for you, then; so much the better.”
source_text: “Oh! you’ve thought better of it, have you?” growled Sikes, marking the tear which trembled in her eye. “All the better for you, you have.”

text: “What I have arranged is that you are to ride into town late to-night; leave here about nine, and stay there awhile, so as to get back about twelve. If nothing has come by eleven to-night, they say there is no chance whatever.”
source_text: “What I’ve arranged is, that you shall ride to town the last thing to-night; leave here about nine, and wait a while there, getting home about twelve. If nothing has been received by eleven to-night, they say there’s no chance at all.”

text: The door opened, and Jim came in and shut it behind him. He looked gaunt and very grave. Poor boy, he was only twenty-two—and already weighted with a family! He needed a new overcoat, and he had no gloves.
source_text: The door opened and Jim stepped in and closed it. He looked thin and very serious. Poor fellow, he was only twenty-two—and to be burdened with a family! He needed a new overcoat and he was without gloves.

text: “Ah!” said Boldwood, with a voice now somewhat more buoyant. “Oh, Troy, if she is the one you prefer most, why did you come between us and wound my happiness?”
source_text: “Ah!” said Boldwood, in a more elastic voice. “Oh, Troy, if you like her best, why then did you step in here and injure my happiness?”

text: He had scored a goal. He had done it neatly. The perambulator, it seemed, did not matter. Though the blow had apparently roused the baby. At the edge of his consciousness there hovered the figure of Mr. Britling saying: "Aunty. You really mustn't wheel the perambulator—_just_ there."
source_text: He'd shot a goal. He'd done it well. The perambulator it seemed didn't matter. Though apparently the impact had awakened the baby. In the margin of his consciousness was the figure of Mr. Britling remarking: "Aunty. You really mustn't wheel the perambulator--_just_ there."

text: “I didn’t take it,” stammered Noah, no longer with his legs stretched out like an independent gentleman, but drawing them up as well as he could beneath his chair; “it was all her doing; you’ve got it now, Charlotte, you know you have.”
source_text: “I didn’t take it,” stammered Noah, no longer stretching out his legs like an independent gentleman, but coiling them up as well as he could under his chair; “it was all her doing; yer’ve got it now, Charlotte, yer know yer have.”

text: “Gad, that’s true!” said the doctor. “Where is he? Show me the way. I’ll look in again as I come down, Mrs. Maylie. That’s the little window he got in by, eh? Well, I could never have believed it!”
source_text: “Gad, that’s true!” said the doctor. “Where is he? Show me the way. I’ll look in again, as I come down, Mrs. Maylie. That’s the little window that he got in at, eh? Well, I couldn’t have believed it!”

text: "Wa-a-a-l," he said with a tremor of laughter, "now who'd have thought it?" and gave himself a deliberately American smile before going down.
source_text: "Wa-a-a-l," he said with a quaver of laughter, "now who'd have thought it?" and smiled a consciously American smile at himself before going down.

text: “We can say the few words we have to say in here, my dear,” said the Jew, flinging open a door on the first floor; “and as there are chinks in the shutters, and we never let our neighbours see lights, we’ll place the candle on the stairs. There!”
source_text: “We can say the few words we’ve got to say in here, my dear,” said the Jew, throwing open a door on the first floor; “and as there are holes in the shutters, and we never show lights to our neighbours, we’ll set the candle on the stairs. There!”

text: “Well—if we manage to arrange it so. I wonder whether Thomasin has been at Blooms-End of late. I hope she has. But probably not, since she is, I believe, expecting to be confined in a month or so. I wish I had thought of that sooner. Poor Mother must indeed be very lonely.”
source_text: “Well—if we make it so. I wonder if Thomasin has been to Blooms-End lately. I hope so. But probably not, as she is, I believe, expecting to be confined in a month or so. I wish I had thought of that before. Poor Mother must indeed be very lonely.”

text: “Well, he knew it early this morning, for I learned it at ten o’clock, when Charley returned. Indeed, he is what I should call a lucky man. What a fool you were, Eustacia!”
source_text: “Well, it was known to him this morning early, for I knew it at ten o’clock, when Charley came back. Now, he is what I call a lucky man. What a fool you were, Eustacia!”

text: “And you must!” she said, with gratitude. “A thousand thanks for your devotion, Gabriel! Good night—I know you are doing all you can for me.”
source_text: “And you better!” she said, gratefully. “Thank you for your devotion, a thousand times, Gabriel! Goodnight—I know you are doing your very best for me.”

text: “I don’t think I do, if I may say what is in my mind. I am not fit to be matched against you to-night. Is the tea-board still there, Lucie? I cannot see.”
source_text: “I don’t think I do like, if I may speak my mind. I am not fit to be pitted against you to-night. Is the teaboard still there, Lucie? I can’t see.”

text: “I call myself the Samson of the firewood guillotine. Look here once more! Loo, loo, loo; loo, loo, loo! And off goes _her_ head! Now, a child. Tickle, tickle; pickle, pickle! And off _its_ head goes. The whole family!”
source_text: “I call myself the Samson of the firewood guillotine. See here again! Loo, loo, loo; Loo, loo, loo! And off _her_ head comes! Now, a child. Tickle, tickle; Pickle, pickle! And off _its_ head comes. All the family!”

text: “Take it easy, Sandy. All I need to know is how _much_ of an ogre is invisible; then I’ll know where to find his vitals. Don’t be frightened—I’ll make short work of these bunco-steerers. Stay right where you are.”
source_text: “You be easy, Sandy. All I need to know is, how _much_ of an ogre is invisible; then I know how to locate his vitals. Don't you be afraid, I will make short work of these bunco-steerers. Stay where you are.”

text: “All right,” she replied. “I think hard. I like the house, this house—mine alone, no pay the rent, seven dollars a month.”
source_text: “Alla right,” she answered. “I thinka da hard. I lika da house, dis house—all mine, no paya da rent, seven dollar da month.”

text: Great God! what a scene has but now unfolded before me! I am still bewildered by the memory of it. I scarcely know whether I shall have strength to recount it; yet the narrative I have set down would be incomplete without this last and terrible catastrophe.
source_text: Great God! what a scene has just taken place! I am yet dizzy with the remembrance of it. I hardly know whether I shall have the power to detail it; yet the tale which I have recorded would be incomplete without this final and wonderful catastrophe.

text: “And what have I heard! What have I heard!” cried Rose. “That the consciousness of his deep disgrace so wrought upon my own father that he avoided all—there, we have said enough, Harry, we have said enough.”
source_text: “And what have I heard! What have I heard!” cried Rose. “That a sense of his deep disgrace so worked upon my own father that he shunned all—there, we have said enough, Harry, we have said enough.”

text: “But I thought—Gabriel, I have been troubled by the fear that I have offended you, and that you are leaving because of it. It has grieved me deeply, and I could not keep myself from coming.”
source_text: “But I thought—Gabriel, I have been uneasy in the belief that I have offended you, and that you are going away on that account. It grieved me very much and I couldn’t help coming.”

text: “If he was coming here, he would have brought the dog,” said Kags, stooping to inspect the animal, which lay panting upon the floor. “Here! Give him some water; he has run himself faint.”
source_text: “If he was coming here, he’d have come with the dog,” said Kags, stooping down to examine the animal, who lay panting on the floor. “Here! Give us some water for him; he has run himself faint.”

text: “And there are two religions going on in the nation now—High Church and High Chapel. And, thinks I, I’ll keep things fair; so I went to High Church in the morning, and High Chapel in the afternoon.”
source_text: “And there’s two religions going on in the nation now—High Church and High Chapel. And, thinks I, I’ll play fair; so I went to High Church in the morning, and High Chapel in the afternoon.”

text: “Did you notice Mr. Boldwood’s doings in church this morning, miss?” Liddy went on, intimating by the remark the direction her thoughts had taken.
source_text: “Did you notice Mr. Boldwood’s doings in church this morning, miss?” Liddy continued, adumbrating by the remark the track her thoughts had taken.

text: “Yes, Miss Eustacia—I will, if I may kiss it as well. A quarter of an hour. And I’ll swear I’ll do my very utmost to let you take my place without anybody’s finding it out. Don’t you think someone might know your tongue, miss?”
source_text: “Yes, Miss Eustacia—I will, if I may kiss it too. A quarter of an hour. And I’ll swear to do the best I can to let you take my place without anybody knowing. Don’t you think somebody might know your tongue, miss?”

text: “It is no weather fit for wasting idle courtesies upon dead folk. They are beyond giving you thanks. Come—it is unprofitable to linger here.”
source_text: “It is not weather meet for doing useless courtesies unto dead folk. They are past thanking you. Come--it is unprofitable to tarry here.”

text: “Well, then, you can unthink it. They bore every strain without difficulty—until the Interdict. Since then, they have only kept up a brave show; in their hearts they are trembling. Set your mind on it—when the armies come, the mask will drop.”
source_text: “Well, then, you may unthink it. They stood every strain easily --until the Interdict. Since then, they merely put on a bold outside--at heart they are quaking. Make up your mind to it --when the armies come, the mask will fall.”

text: “That’s right,” he said with gratitude, “where I am. But where am I at—I mean, where am I? Oh, yes, in the chart-room. Well, some people—”
source_text: “That’s right,” he said gratefully, “where I am. But where am I at—I mean, where am I? Oh, yes, in the chart-room. Well, some people—”

text: “I hope you don’t mean to say, sir,” said Mr. Giles, trembling all the while, “that he is going to die. If I believed that, I should never know happiness again. I would not cut a boy off—no, not even Brittles here—not for all the plate in the county, sir.”
source_text: “I hope you don’t mean to say, sir,” said Mr. Giles, trembling, “that he’s going to die. If I thought it, I should never be happy again. I wouldn’t cut a boy off: no, not even Brittles here; not for all the plate in the county, sir.”

text: “I tell you what it is,” said Sikes; “if you haven’t caught the fever, and got it coming on now, there’s something more than common in the wind, and something dangerous too. You’re not a-going to— No, damme! you wouldn’t do that!”
source_text: “I tell you wot it is,” said Sikes; “if you haven’t caught the fever, and got it comin’ on, now, there’s something more than usual in the wind, and something dangerous too. You’re not a-going to—. No, damme! you wouldn’t do that!”

text: “She’s a honour to her sex,” said Mr. Sikes, as he filled his glass and brought his huge fist down upon the table. “Here’s her health, and may they all be like her!”
source_text: “She’s a honour to her sex,” said Mr. Sikes, filling his glass, and smiting the table with his enormous fist. “Here’s her health, and wishing they was all like her!”

text: “Go on!” cried the Dodger. “What, where’s your spirit? Don’t you feel any pride in yourself? Would you go and make yourself dependent on your friends?”
source_text: “Go!” exclaimed the Dodger. “Why, where’s your spirit? Don’t you take any pride out of yourself? Would you go and be dependent on your friends?”

text: “I tossed them aside—it was only a passing annoyance. What a fool I am! Here—come and help me look for them—we must find them again.”
source_text: “I threw them away—it was a momentary irritation. What a fool I am! Here—come and help me to look for them—we must find them again.”

text: “Have no fear! I shall soon be beyond the reach of harming you, and the others will soon be far from here, God willing! Now, fetch help and take me to the coach.”
source_text: “Have no fear! I shall soon be out of the way of harming you, and the rest will soon be far from here, please God! Now, get assistance and take me to the coach.”

text: “I hadn’t a soul to look after the shop,” replied the man. “Every one who might have given me help had gone off in the pursuit. I couldn’t get hold of anybody until five minutes ago, and I’ve been running here all the way.”
source_text: “I hadn’t a soul to mind the shop,” replied the man. “Everybody who could have helped me, had joined in the pursuit. I could get nobody till five minutes ago; and I’ve run here all the way.”

text: “Bad fortune!” cries The Vengeance, stamping her foot upon the chair, “and there are the tumbrils! And Evrémonde will be dispatched in a wink, and she not here! See her knitting in my hand, and her empty chair waiting for her. I cry out with vexation and disappointment!”
source_text: “Bad Fortune!” cries The Vengeance, stamping her foot in the chair, “and here are the tumbrils! And Evrémonde will be despatched in a wink, and she not here! See her knitting in my hand, and her empty chair ready for her. I cry with vexation and disappointment!”

text: “What the devil induced you to keep lingering there in the rain?” said Monks, turning round and addressing Bumble, after he had bolted the door behind them.
source_text: “What the devil made you stand lingering there, in the wet?” said Monks, turning round, and addressing Bumble, after he had bolted the door behind them.

text: “I have had the happiness, Doctor Manette, of being so much at home here,” he at last began, “for some year and a half, that I trust the subject upon which I am about to venture may not--”
source_text: “I have had the happiness, Doctor Manette, of being so intimate here,” so he at length began, “for some year and a half, that I hope the topic on which I am about to touch may not--”

text: “Perhaps you dislike the rudeness of my rebuking you, for I know it is rude; but I thought it might do some good.”
source_text: “Perhaps you don’t like the rudeness of my reprimanding you, for I know it is rudeness; but I thought it would do good.”

text: “And that tale of the black man who murdered his wife Desdemona? It is a pleasingly dismal one, and would suit you most excellently just now.”
source_text: “And that story of the black man, who murdered his wife Desdemona? It is a nice dismal one that would suit you excellent just now.”

text: “Colours for the counties,” said Wilkins, “and if there’s no coloured cloth to be had, there’s always red flannel. Anything is preferable to leaving the crowd of people to go roaming about....”
source_text: "Colours for the counties," said Wilkins, "and if there isn't coloured cloth to be got there's--red flannel. Anything is better than leaving the mass of people to mob about...."

text: She hid her face in her hands. “Oh, it is a mistake!” she groaned. “And some day he will regret it, and remember me!”
source_text: She covered her face with her hands. “O, it is a mistake!” she groaned. “And he will rue it some day, and think of me!”

text: --and set the green fire blazing! After waiting only forty seconds this time, I flung my arms wide and hurled forth the crushing syllables of that word among words:
source_text: --and lit up the green fire! After waiting only forty seconds this time, I spread my arms abroad and thundered out the devastating syllables of this word of words:

text: “‘Heaven forfend! Even if you were indeed guilty, for that can only reduce you to despair, and not impel you toward virtue. I too am wretched; I and my family have been condemned, though innocent; judge, then, whether I can be insensible to your afflictions.’”
source_text: “‘Heaven forbid! Even if you were really criminal, for that can only drive you to desperation, and not instigate you to virtue. I also am unfortunate; I and my family have been condemned, although innocent; judge, therefore, if I do not feel for your misfortunes.’

text: “Stranger things than that will happen when it does arrive,” replied madame. “I have them both here, beyond question; and they are both here for their own deserts; that is enough.”
source_text: “Stranger things than that will happen when it does come,” answered madame. “I have them both here, of a certainty; and they are both here for their merits; that is enough.”

text: “Stay quietly here until such a document is prepared, and then come with me to whatever place I may judge most suitable for the purpose of signing it?”
source_text: “Remain quietly here, until such a document is drawn up, and proceed with me to such a place as I may deem most advisable, for the purpose of attesting it?”

text: There was something in the fact that he had been taken prisoner. “That other officer”—Mr. Direck’s officer, that was—“had been lying there for days.” Teddy had been shot through the upper arm and stunned by a falling beam. When he came to himself, he was disarmed, with a German standing over him....
source_text: There was something about his being taken prisoner. "That other officer"--that was Mr. Direck's officer--"had been lying there for days." Teddy had been shot through the upper arm, and stunned by a falling beam. When he came to he was disarmed, with a German standing over him....

text: Venn made as though he would withdraw to the kitchen, when Thomasin said, with a pleasant sharpness, and continuing at her sewing, “Of course you must sit down here. And where is your fifty-cow dairy situated, Mr. Venn?”
source_text: Venn moved as if he would retire to the kitchen, when Thomasin said with pleasant pertness as she went on with some sewing, “Of course you must sit down here. And where does your fifty-cow dairy lie, Mr. Venn?”

text: “Indeed! You are a fine fellow to be objecting and advising!” cried Mr. Lorry. “You wish you were going yourself? And you, a Frenchman by birth? Truly, you are a sagacious counsellor.”
source_text: “Indeed! You are a pretty fellow to object and advise!” exclaimed Mr. Lorry. “You wish you were going yourself? And you a Frenchman born? You are a wise counsellor.”

text: “The unfortunate circumstances—if you had happened upon us on any other day,” Mr. Ford began with smooth politeness, only to be cut off by Mr. Ends, whose peevish eyes bore out the sharpness of his temper.
source_text: “The unfortunate circumstances—if you had chanced here any other day,” Mr. Ford began suavely, only to be interrupted by Mr. Ends, whose cranky eyes justified themselves in his shortness of temper.

text: “She promises that you shall—quite natural,” said the scheming lover, every nerve in him stirring at the presumption Liddy’s words seemed to authorize—that his darling had even considered marrying again.
source_text: “She promises that you shall—quite natural,” said the strategic lover, throbbing throughout him at the presumption which Liddy’s words appeared to warrant—that his darling had thought of re-marriage.

text: “You remember that five-dollar bill you gave me when I didn’t have the fare for the car? Well, there it is, with ninety-nine brothers of different ages, though all of the same size.”
source_text: “You remember that fiver you gave me the time I didn’t have carfare? Well, there it is, with ninety-nine brothers of different ages but all of the same size.”

text: “You could not discover the place to which these people had gone,” said Monks, “but where friendship breaks down, hatred will often make a passage. My mother found it, after a year of crafty searching—ay, and found the child.”
source_text: “You couldn’t find the spot to which these people had repaired,” said Monks, “but where friendship fails, hatred will often force a way. My mother found it, after a year of cunning search—ay, and found the child.”

text: “You are a sensible little woman,” said Mrs. Yeobright, smiling. “I wish you and he—no, I don’t wish for anything. There, it is nine o’clock,” she broke off, as a whizzing and a dinging sounded below.
source_text: “You are a practical little woman,” said Mrs. Yeobright, smiling. “I wish you and he—no, I don’t wish anything. There, it is nine o’clock,” she interrupted, hearing a whizz and a dinging downstairs.

text: “Why have you been staying here all these days without telling me? You have left your home. I am afraid that I may be to blame for this?”
source_text: “Why have you been living here all these days without telling me? You have left your home. I fear I am something to blame in this?”

text: “Ah, perchance, my head being so distraught by the manifold matters unto which pertain the confusions of these but lately adventured haps and fortunes, whereby not I alone nor you alone, but every one of us, it seemeth to me--”
source_text: “Ah, peradventure my head being distraught by the manifold matters whereunto the confusions of these but late adventured haps and fortunings whereby not I alone nor you alone, but every each of us, meseemeth--”

text: Probably some friend of Lady Frensham’s, he supposed. Yet—the notion of Zeppelins over London did strike Mr. Britling as distinctly absurd. He would not have cared to be caught saying so himself.... There had never been Zeppelins over London. They were merely gas-bags....
source_text: Probably a friend of Lady Frensham's, he thought. Still--the idea of Zeppelins over London did seem rather ridiculous to Mr. Britling. He would not have liked to have been caught talking of it himself.... There never had been Zeppelins over London. They were gas bags....

text: “True truth, Mr. Darnay, believe me! I have wandered from my point; I was speaking of our being friends. Now you know me; you know I am incapable of all those loftier and nobler flights of men. If you doubt it, ask Stryver, and he will tell you as much.”
source_text: “Genuine truth, Mr. Darnay, trust me! I have gone aside from my purpose; I was speaking about our being friends. Now, you know me; you know I am incapable of all the higher and better flights of men. If you doubt it, ask Stryver, and he’ll tell you so.”

text: Spring moved forward with rapid step; the weather grew fair, and the heavens were without a cloud. It astonished me that what had once been a desert, wild and melancholy, should now be adorned with the fairest flowers and freshest verdure. My senses were pleased and revived by a thousand delightful fragrances and a thousand lovely sights.
source_text: “Spring advanced rapidly; the weather became fine and the skies cloudless. It surprised me that what before was desert and gloomy should now bloom with the most beautiful flowers and verdure. My senses were gratified and refreshed by a thousand scents of delight and a thousand sights of beauty.

text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin said, shaking his hand and motioning him toward the lone chair, while he himself settled on the bed. “But how did you know where I lived?”
source_text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin answered, shaking hands and waving him to the solitary chair, himself taking to the bed. “But how did you know where I lived?”

text: Venn was greatly astonished by this confession, though he did not make it very plain; such displays may attend remarks that are but a step beyond what is expected, yet they are commonly reserved in more intricate instances of two steps and more. “Indeed, miss,” he replied.
source_text: Venn felt much astonishment at this avowal, though he did not show it clearly; that exhibition may greet remarks which are one remove from expectation, but it is usually withheld in complicated cases of two removes and upwards. “Indeed, miss,” he replied.

text: “I’ll tell you what,” whispered the gentleman to Mr. Brownlow; “he will not come to you to-morrow morning. I saw him falter. He is deceiving you, my good friend.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you what,” whispered that gentleman to Mr. Brownlow; “he won’t come up to you tomorrow morning. I saw him hesitate. He is deceiving you, my good friend.”

text: [*All the particulars regarding the hermits in this chapter are taken from Lecky—but with heavy modification. This being not a history, but only a tale, most of the historian's frank details were too strong to be reproduced in it.—_Editor_]
source_text: [*All the details concerning the hermits, in this chapter, are from Lecky--but greatly modified. This book not being a history but only a tale, the majority of the historian's frank details were too strong for reproduction in it.--_Editor_]

text: “And she made up her mind that the wedding must be over before you got back,” said Mrs. Yeobright, “so that there should be no chance of her meeting you, and going through a very painful time. That is why she has gone to him; they have settled to be married this morning.”
source_text: “And she determined that the wedding should be over before you came back,” said Mrs. Yeobright, “that there might be no chance of her meeting you, and having a very painful time of it. That’s why she has gone to him; they have arranged to be married this morning.”

text: “My wife appears to believe I’m a burglar,” he said, with reckless composure. “I never heard anyone call her mad; so it must be I. And if I am mad, then they can’t do anything to me for killing you two fools in my madness.”
source_text: “My wife seems to think I am a burglar,” he said, recklessly. “I never heard of her being crazy; therefore I must be. And if I’m crazy, they can’t do anything to me for killing you two fools in my madness.”

text: “She, above all,” said Ernest, “needs consolation; she blamed herself for having brought about my brother’s death, and that left her in great misery. But now that the murderer has been found—”
source_text: “She most of all,” said Ernest, “requires consolation; she accused herself of having caused the death of my brother, and that made her very wretched. But since the murderer has been discovered—”

text: “What do you think now?” said the Jew, once they had made their way back into the passage. “Apart from ourselves, there isn’t a soul in the house except Toby and the boys; and they’re safe enough. Look here!”
source_text: “What do you think now?” said the Jew, when they had regained the passage. “Besides ourselves, there’s not a creature in the house except Toby and the boys; and they’re safe enough. See here!”

text: “I have considered it my duty to come today. A new proposal has been made to me, and it has somewhat astonished me. It will have a great bearing on Thomasin; and I have determined that it should at least be spoken of to you.”
source_text: “I have thought it my duty to call today. A new proposal has been made to me, which has rather astonished me. It will affect Thomasin greatly; and I have decided that it should at least be mentioned to you.”

text: “What is this?” cried Mr. Lorry, out of breath and bewildered. “What is the matter? Lucie! Manette! What has happened? What has brought you here? What is it?”
source_text: “What is this?” cried Mr. Lorry, breathless and confused. “What is the matter? Lucie! Manette! What has happened? What has brought you here? What is it?”

text: “You have seen both dolls and birds to-day,” said Madame Defarge, with a motion of her hand toward the spot where they had last been visible; “now, go home!”
source_text: “You have seen both dolls and birds to-day,” said Madame Defarge, with a wave of her hand towards the place where they had last been apparent; “now, go home!”

text: “And so my entire plan is upset! It is very inconvenient that you refuse to assist my family even to the slight degree of saying plainly that you will have nothing to do with us.”
source_text: “And so my whole plan is interfered with! It is very inconvenient that you refuse to help my family even to the small extent of saying distinctly you will have nothing to do with us.”

text: “You do not go on with your story of your matrimonial intentions,” returned Carton, with an indifferent air; “I wish you would stick to that. As for me—will you never understand that I am incorrigible?”
source_text: “You don’t get on with your account of your matrimonial intentions,” answered Carton, with a careless air; “I wish you would keep to that. As to me--will you never understand that I am incorrigible?”

text: Thomasin paused for a moment, and then placed the baby in Venn’s hands. “Don’t squeeze her, Diggory,” she said, “or hurt her little arm; and keep the cloak close over her like this, so that the rain may not drop upon her face.”
source_text: Thomasin hesitated a moment, and then delivered the baby into Venn’s hands. “Don’t squeeze her, Diggory,” she said, “or hurt her little arm; and keep the cloak close over her like this, so that the rain may not drop in her face.”

text: “But she has a will—not to mention a temper—and I shall be nothing better than a slave to her. I could do anything at all with poor Fanny Robin.”
source_text: “But she has a will—not to say a temper, and I shall be a mere slave to her. I could do anything with poor Fanny Robin.”

text: “No, miss.... No, I say; though, to be sure, it might be Sergeant Troy come home on furlough, though I have not seen him. He was here once in that fashion when the regiment was at Casterbridge.”
source_text: “No, miss.... No, I say; but really it might be Sergeant Troy home on furlough, though I have not seen him. He was here once in that way when the regiment was at Casterbridge.”

text: “You handed ’em over to him, you red-headed sneak, slippin’ in behind the push there,” Martin went on, flingin’ the knuckles into the water. “I seen you, an’ I was figurin’ what you was up to. If you ever try a thing like that again, I’ll beat cheh to death. Do you understand?”
source_text: “You passed ’em to him, you red-head sneakin’ in behind the push there,” Martin went on, as he tossed the knuckles into the water. “I seen you, an’ I was wonderin’ what you was up to. If you try anything like that again, I’ll beat cheh to death. Understand?”

text: “It would bring our adventure to an end before it had scarcely begun; and we, being unarmed, could make no stand against that armed band. If we are to bring our enterprise to success, we must not merely seem peasants, but conduct ourselves as peasants.”
source_text: “It would end our adventures at the very start; and we, being without weapons, could do nothing with that armed gang. If we are going to succeed in our emprise, we must not only look the peasant but act the peasant.”

text: “I thought we might learn something today,” Coggan went on. “I heard wheels go by my door soon after dark—you were away somewhere.” He cast a look at Gabriel. “Good heavens above us, Oak, how white you are! You look as if you were a corpse!”
source_text: “I fancied we should know something to-day,” continued Coggan. “I heard wheels pass my door just after dark—you were out somewhere.” He glanced round upon Gabriel. “Good heavens above us, Oak, how white your face is; you look like a corpse!”

text: “Well—perhaps it is better to keep silence; but I cannot go any farther than that. I cannot flatter, and if my standing here is to be maintained only by soothing him, then my standing must be forfeited.”
source_text: “Well—perhaps it is best to be silent; but I can’t go further than that. I can’t flatter, and if my place here is only to be kept by smoothing him down, my place must be lost.”

text: Elizabeth watched my agitation for some time in a timid and anxious silence; yet there was something in my look that conveyed terror to her, and, trembling, she asked, “What is it that disturbs you, my dear Victor? What is it you fear?”
source_text: Elizabeth observed my agitation for some time in timid and fearful silence, but there was something in my glance which communicated terror to her, and trembling, she asked, “What is it that agitates you, my dear Victor? What is it you fear?”

text: “Meanwhile,” said the Marquis, “I shall preserve the family’s honour and tranquillity, if you will not. But you must be wearied. Shall we conclude our conference for the night?”
source_text: “Meanwhile,” said the Marquis, “I will preserve the honour and repose of the family, if you will not. But you must be fatigued. Shall we terminate our conference for the night?”

text: “If they have—I do not see how they can; but if they have—any allusion to me,” said Rose, “pray let me hear it at another time. I have neither strength nor spirits for it now.”
source_text: “If they have—I do not know how they can, but if they have—any reference to me,” said Rose, “pray let me hear them at some other time. I have not strength or spirits now.”

text: “Oho! wouldn’t it?” roared the champion of the root of evil. “Tell me where your exclusive circles would be if the first Astor hadn’t had the money to pay for his steerage passage over?”
source_text: “Oho! won’t it?” thundered the champion of the root of evil. “You tell me where your exclusive circles would be if the first Astor hadn’t had the money to pay for his steerage passage over?”

text: “Why didn’t you choose a pleasant subject?” she was saying. “We know there are ugly things in the world, but that is no reason—”
source_text: “Why didn’t you select a nice subject?” she was saying. “We know there are nasty things in the world, but that is no reason—”

text: “My poor wife!” he said. “My dear wife, if it were not for you—I think I could kill myself tonight. Don’t cry, my dear. Don’t, don’t cry. You do not know how much comfort you give me. You do not know how much you help me.”
source_text: "My poor wife!" he said, "my dear wife. If it were not for you--I think I could kill myself to-night. Don't cry, my dear. Don't, don't cry. You do not know how you comfort me. You do not know how you help me."

text: “Oh, don’t tell me what you were going to say,” Mrs. Sowerberry broke in. “I am nobody; do not consult me, pray. _I_ have no wish to force myself into your secrets.” As Mrs. Sowerberry spoke, she gave a hysterical laugh, which seemed likely to have violent consequences.
source_text: “Oh, don’t tell me what you were going to say,” interposed Mrs. Sowerberry. “I am nobody; don’t consult me, pray. _I_ don’t want to intrude upon your secrets.” As Mrs. Sowerberry said this, she gave an hysterical laugh, which threatened violent consequences.

text: He settled his cheeks in his hands and stayed that way for a long while. His eyes looked fixedly before him, though at nothing. His thoughts drifted, widened, and grew dim. At last he brought his mind back to that final notion. “Just send the fiddle—without a word.”
source_text: He rested his cheeks between his hands, and remained so for a long time. His eyes stared unseeingly. His thoughts wandered and spread and faded. At length he recalled his mind to that last idea. "Just send the fiddle--without a word."

text: “Yes, but this is different,” Martin protested. “She—I—well, you know how it is: perhaps she won’t even be there. She goes to the university.”
source_text: “Yes, but this is different,” Martin objected. “She—I—well, you see, it’s this way: maybe she won’t be there. She goes to the university.”

text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you imagine that my first thought would not turn at once to those dear, dear friends whom I love, and who are so worthy of my affection?”
source_text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you suppose that my first thought would not fly towards those dear, dear friends whom I love and who are so deserving of my love?”
